evidence: Dependency graph is acyclic and layered: src/segments/ depends on src/ and src/campaigns/, src/campaigns/ depends on src/types.ts only, src/ depends on src/segments/ only through layout.ts imports
src/types.ts is the most-imported module (11 importers) but serves a legitimate shared-types role with no behavioral coupling
src/colors.ts (6 importers) is a pure utility with no mutable state — appropriate hub
layout.ts hardcodes imports of all 5 segments, creating a registration coupling where adding a segment requires editing layout.ts

Bidirectional coupling between src/ and src/segments/ directories: src/segments/ imports from src/ (11 edges), src/ imports from src/segments/ (5 edges) — but the directions are layered (types/colors downward, segment instances upward to layout)
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: layout.ts hardcodes all segment imports creating a manual registration coupling; the bidirectional directory-level coupling between src/ and src/segments/ is structurally sound but could become friction if segments grow

evidence: Directory structure maps directly to domain: src/segments/ for rendering units, src/campaigns/ for promotional logic, src/ root for orchestration (index, layout, config, cli) and shared utilities (colors, types)
Each segment file has a single responsibility: one Segment export that owns its rendering
Campaigns subsystem is self-contained: data.ts holds definitions, engine.ts evaluates state — only consumed by campaign segment
Entry points are clear and minimal: cli.ts for interactive use, index.ts for piped stdin, both converge on layout.ts render()
14 files, 971 LOC — appropriately sized for the scope; no bloated files
Previously flagged dead progress bar logic in campaign.ts has been cleaned up
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: cli.ts is flagged as orphaned (87 LOC, 0 importers) because it is an entry point loaded via dynamic import from itself — scanner artifact, not a real issue. The help text in cli.ts does not document the 'promo' or 'usage' segments, showing some doc/reality drift.

evidence: All source files use consistent camelCase naming for functions and variables across all directories
All segments use named exports of the pattern `export const xyzSegment: Segment`
All segments follow the same structural protocol: id, priority, enabled(), render() returning Block
Campaign segment uses id 'promo' while file is campaign.ts and export is campaignSegment — naming inconsistency persists
Previously flagged priority mismatch (3 of 5 segments returning different Block.priority than Segment.priority) has been resolved — all segments now return consistent values
Export patterns are uniform across sibling modules: all segments/ files use named exports, all src/ root files use named exports
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: The campaign/promo naming inconsistency creates a navigation mismatch where a developer looking for the 'promo' segment by searching for promo.ts would not find campaign.ts

evidence: The codebase uses two error strategies: try-catch-swallow for non-critical operations (terminal width detection, config loading, transcript parsing) and return-null for optional data (getGitInfo, getActiveCampaign)
index.ts outer catch now writes diagnostics to stderr (previously flagged issue is resolved)
effort.ts consistently documents catch rationale with inline comments (/* skip malformed lines */, /* file not readable */, /* no settings */)
index.ts inner catches (lines 28, 33), config.ts catch (line 19), and git.ts catch (line 26) are bare with no rationale comment
Error strategies by layer: CLI boundary (cli.ts) uses console.error + process.exit, piped entry (index.ts) uses stderr + blank stdout, internal modules swallow or return null
No error context propagation anywhere — all catches are terminal (swallow or return null), which is appropriate for a status line tool where any failure should degrade gracefully
campaignSegment export in campaign.ts uses id 'promo', creating a vocabulary split: file name says 'campaign', id says 'promo', DEFAULT_ORDER references 'promo' — developers must know both terms

resetCountdown in usage.ts reads as an imperative action (reset the countdown) but is actually a pure formatter converting epoch seconds to human-readable countdown strings — inconsistent with formatTokens and formatCountdown naming convention elsewhere
Most names are precise and domain-aligned: formatTokens, formatCountdown, visibleLength, padRight, getTimeInTz, tildeDir, getGitInfo, miniBar all clearly communicate their purpose
Segment interface names (contextSegment, modelSegment, gitSegment, usageSegment) follow a consistent {domain}Segment pattern that is immediately recognizable
Helper function names in engine.ts (isWeekend, getDowNum, formatCountdown) are concise and accurate
Type names (StatusLineData, Block, Segment, Campaign, CampaignStatus, StatusBlocksConfig) are domain-aligned and descriptive
Variable names like barColor, peakStartSecs, peakEndSecs, secsUntilPeak are precise and units-annotated
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: The campaign/promo vocabulary split requires developers to mentally map between two terms for the same concept. resetCountdown's imperative-sounding name breaks the format* convention established elsewhere.

evidence: The Segment interface has 5 concrete implementations, all used polymorphically in layout.ts via ALL_SEGMENTS array — this is a genuine abstraction that pays for itself
Block is the boundary contract between segments and layout — segments produce Blocks, layout consumes them, with no leaky abstraction between the two
No pass-through wrappers exist: every function adds behavior, validation, or transformation
No large option bags are passed through layers — StatusLineData flows from index.ts to segments but each segment destructures only what it needs
The CampaignStatus interface is used at the boundary between engine.ts and campaign.ts segment, with the segment consuming state, countdown, and progress fields directly
colors.ts exports 4 concrete functions (color, visibleLength, padRight, truncate-now-private) each with distinct behavior — no ceremony layer

Single-use finding for context.ts (only imported by layout.ts) is expected: each segment is consumed only by the layout orchestrator, this is the intended architecture, not a sign of unnecessary abstraction
Single-use finding for campaign.ts (only imported by layout.ts) is same pattern — all segments are single-use by design
src/index.ts indirection_hotspot (max_chain_depth: 2, chain_count: 8) reflects the natural data flow: stdin parse → loadConfig → render, which is appropriate for an entry point
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: The StatusBlocksConfig type has fields (separator, showCampaigns) that appear unused in the current codebase, representing minor speculative surface area. The RowGroup type alias is used only within layout.ts and could be inlined, though this is marginal.

evidence: package.json has zero production dependencies — the project uses only Node.js built-in modules (fs, path, os, child_process, Intl)
devDependencies are minimal and appropriate: typescript ^5.7.0 for compilation, @types/node ^22.0.0 for type definitions
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose: all date/time handling uses native Date and Intl.DateTimeFormat, all file I/O uses fs, all process execution uses child_process
No circular dependency cycles visible in the import graph: src/ <- src/segments/ and src/ <- src/campaigns/ are clean one-directional flows
No heavy dependencies pulled in for light use — the codebase avoids external packages entirely
The coupling_matrix shows clean directional flow: segments -> src (11 edges), src -> segments (5 edges for imports), campaigns -> src (2 edges), segments -> campaigns (1 edge)
ESM-only module system with no CommonJS/ESM interop issues
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: The bidirectional edge between src/ and src/segments/ (11 edges segments->src, 5 edges src->segments) is architecturally intentional (orchestrator imports plugins, plugins import shared types/utils) but creates a technical bidirectional coupling. This is minor and appropriate for a 14-file codebase.

evidence: layout.ts render() has been refactored: findOptimalAssignment and materializeAssignment are now extracted, making render() a clean 24-line orchestrator — previously flagged issue is resolved
campaign.ts render() no longer has dead bar/lines variables or stale comments — previously flagged issue is resolved

model.ts render() lines 58-81: the 3-part line2 gap distribution uses 12+ local variables (dot, dotLen, eLen, dLen, vLen, contentWidth, spareWithDots, clamped, g, extra, g1-g4) for manual gap arithmetic with a branching threshold (spareWithDots <= 12) — this remains complex and hard to follow

findOptimalAssignment (layout.ts lines 73-128): brute-force enumeration with O(n^n) complexity uses imperative C-style loops with pre-allocated arrays (rowOf, seen, rowRW), manual base-N digit extraction, and inline row width computation — correct but dense, requiring careful reading to understand the optimization

engine.ts getActiveCampaign() (lines 49-137): 88-line function mixing time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation with 5 early returns and deeply nested conditionals — the weekend progress calculation (lines 72-80) is particularly hard to follow with daysSinceFri/secsSinceFriPeak/totalWeekendSecs arithmetic
Segment render functions (context.ts, git.ts, usage.ts) are concise and direct — each builds 2 lines of output with clear variable names and minimal branching
colors.ts truncate() uses an efficient character-by-character walk with ANSI-skip logic — tight and intention-revealing despite the low-level pointer manipulation
effort.ts resolveEffort() has clean two-source fallback structure with caching, but the inner JSONL parsing loop (lines 24-39) uses deeply nested try/catch with a complex text extraction chain
Segment interface provides a clean contract: each segment implements id, priority, enabled(), render() and returns a Block — layout.ts consumes these uniformly without per-segment conditionals
Data flows linearly: index.ts parses stdin JSON, passes StatusLineData to render(), which orchestrates segments and materializes rows — no tangled back-and-forth callbacks
layout.ts row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5 embeds an unexplained policy constant directly in orchestration logic, mixing concern of 'notification panel sizing' with 'block placement'
Campaign engine boundary is clean: campaign.ts calls getActiveCampaign() from engine.ts and receives a typed CampaignStatus — translation happens in one place
Config loading is a single call from index.ts with env var override logic contained entirely within config.ts — no scattered config reads across modules
effort.ts is called from model.ts render() and encapsulates its own caching and multi-source resolution — the boundary is explicit (transcriptPath in, EffortLevel out)
Zero test files exist: no *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, or __tests__/ directories anywhere in the repository
No test framework configured: package.json has no test runner (vitest, jest, mocha) in devDependencies
No test script: package.json scripts has build, dev, preview but no 'test' entry
14 production files totaling 971 LOC have zero automated test coverage
colors.ts (6 importers, most-imported utility) has no tests for ANSI-aware visibleLength, padRight, or truncate — these are pure functions ideal for unit testing
campaigns/engine.ts (137 LOC) performs timezone-aware date arithmetic with Intl.DateTimeFormat, weekend detection, and peak/off-peak state transitions — all untested
layout.ts (179 LOC) contains a combinatorial bin-packing optimizer (findOptimalAssignment) with complex row assignment logic — untested
effort.ts parses JSONL transcript files with regex matching and multi-source fallback — untested
The 'preview' command in cli.ts provides visual spot-checking but is not an automated test
All 5 segments implement the same Segment interface consistently: id (string), priority (number), enabled(data) (boolean), render(data, allocWidth) (Block) — uniform API shape across siblings
All public functions are synchronous — no mixed sync/async in any module's API surface
No function in the public API exceeds 5 parameters — render(data, termWidth, config) has 3, segment render(data, allocWidth) has 2
Previously reported issue of Block.priority contradicting Segment.priority has been fixed — model.ts, campaign.ts, and usage.ts now return matching priority values
Previously reported dead export truncate() in colors.ts has been fixed — it is now module-private
colors.ts exports a coherent set: color(), c (constants), visibleLength(), padRight() — all ANSI-related, all synchronous, consistent naming
loadConfig() in config.ts has a single return type (StatusBlocksConfig) with no overloads or variant signatures
getActiveCampaign() returns CampaignStatus | null — clear nullable contract without overloads
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The Segment interface's render() method has inconsistent parameter usage — some segments use allocWidth (model.ts), others ignore it entirely (campaign.ts, git.ts, context.ts, usage.ts), creating a contract where the parameter exists but is meaningless for most implementations. Additionally, the enabled() method in campaign.ts calls the expensive getActiveCampaign() which is then called again in render(), suggesting the API boundary could better support a check-then-render pattern.

evidence: This is a CLI status-line renderer — it reads JSON from stdin and writes ANSI-formatted text to stdout. There are no HTTP routes, no API endpoints, no user authentication, no database access, and no protected resources
The codebase has no auth decorators, middleware, permission checks, or trust boundaries because none are needed for its architecture
File reads are limited to config (~/.claude-statusblocks.json), settings (~/.claude/settings.json), and transcript JSONL — all local user files with OS-level permissions
The only subprocess calls are git commands and terminal size detection (stty, tput) — standard CLI tool patterns with no auth implications
No network calls, no service roles, no admin bypasses, no RLS patterns exist
Code is purpose-driven with minimal ceremony across all 14 files

Comments are sparse (5.7% comment ratio) and mostly explain WHY, not WHAT — e.g., '// Claude Code's outer paddingX: 2 on each side' in layout.ts, '// Strip "(1M context)" etc. from display name' in model.ts
No restating comments echoing code found across segments or core modules
No debug/trace logging in production code paths — console.log/error usage is confined to cli.ts for intentional user-facing CLI output (init, preview, help)
No pass-through wrapper functions — all functions add logic or transformation
No docstring bloat — the few JSDoc comments are concise and on non-trivial functions (e.g., engine.ts getActiveCampaign, layout.ts findOptimalAssignment)
No generic names like handleData/processItem in domain code; names are domain-specific (formatTokens, tildeDir, getGitInfo, miniBar, resetCountdown)
Previously reported issue (stale thinking-out-loud comments and dead code in campaign.ts) has been fixed — campaign.ts render() now cleanly builds lines without dead intermediate computation
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: cli.ts has elevated log density (4.0 per function) with raw ANSI escape sequences inlined in console.log calls rather than using the project's own color() utility; this creates a minor style inconsistency that echoes a 'copied template' pattern rather than idiomatic project code.

evidence: All 14 source files are TypeScript — no mixed JS/TS modules
Consistent ESM throughout: 'type: module' in package.json, all imports use .js extensions, no require() calls anywhere
No deprecated symbols or @deprecated markers found in the codebase
No compatibility shims or legacy wrappers present
No stale migration TODOs referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after'
No coexisting API patterns (single HTTP approach, single state management pattern, single component style)
Migration signal detected: 1 var vs 13 let/const — but this is negligible residue rather than an incomplete migration pattern
Rate limits data was migrated from external API fetch to native StatusLineData.rate_limits field (commit e4e7ddf), and the migration is complete with no residual code paths
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: One residual var usage exists alongside 13 let/const declarations, though this is trivially minor and may be in a specific context where var is intentional.

evidence: Three directories with clear purpose: src/ (core infrastructure), src/segments/ (rendering units), src/campaigns/ (promotional rate logic)
Directory profiles show coherent groupings: src/ has 7 files (530 LOC, avg fan-in 3.1), src/segments/ has 5 files (281 LOC, avg fan-in 1.0), src/campaigns/ has 2 files (160 LOC, avg fan-in 1.0)

Coupling matrix shows clean dependency direction: segments/ imports from src/ (11 edges), src/ imports from segments/ (5 edges for segment registration), campaigns/ imports from src/ (2 edges for types), segments/ imports from campaigns/ (1 edge for campaign segment)

The bidirectional coupling between src/ and src/segments/ (11 down, 5 up) is structurally necessary: segments import types/colors from src/, while layout.ts in src/ imports segment implementations for registration
Total codebase is 14 files / 971 LOC — well under the 20-file threshold where flat structure is appropriate, making any reorganization suggestions premature
No flat directory overload: src/ has 7 files (all with distinct responsibilities — cli, colors, config, effort, index, layout, types), segments/ has 5 files (one per segment), campaigns/ has 2 files
Root files (types.ts with 11 importers, colors.ts with 6) are genuinely core — high fan-in across multiple subdirectories, appropriate at the src/ root level
Directory names accurately reflect contained responsibilities: segments/ contains segment implementations, campaigns/ contains campaign data and engine
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: The bidirectional coupling between src/ and src/segments/ is a mild structural signal — layout.ts must import all segment implementations, creating an upward dependency from infrastructure to feature modules. For a 5-segment codebase this is pragmatic, but at scale it would warrant a registration pattern.

evidence: CONFIG_PATH in config.ts (line 6) computes join(homedir(), '.claude-statusblocks.json') at import time — this bakes the home directory into a module-level constant, creating a stale snapshot that cannot be overridden in tests
SETTINGS_PATH in cli.ts (line 10) computes join(homedir(), '.claude', 'settings.json') at import time — same stale snapshot pattern

effort.ts correctly resolves homedir() at call time inside resolveEffort() (line 46: const configDir = process.env.CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR || join(homedir(), '.claude')), establishing a pattern the other files do not follow
gitCache in git.ts (line 5) is a module-level mutable singleton, but its TTL-based invalidation (5s) and single-consumer usage make this pragmatic rather than problematic
effort.ts cache (line 9) is another module-level mutable singleton with TTL-based invalidation — same pragmatic pattern as gitCache
No import-time side effects for DB connections, file I/O, or network calls at module scope
No circular init dependencies — import graph is acyclic (segments → src, campaigns → src types)
No environment variable reads at import time except implicitly through homedir() calls in CONFIG_PATH and SETTINGS_PATH
MOCK_DATA in cli.ts uses process.cwd() and Date.now() at import time (lines 14, 19-20), but cli.ts is an entry point script, not an imported module, so this has no init-ordering impact

getActiveCampaign() in engine.ts (88 lines) mixes time-zone resolution, day-of-week classification, peak/off-peak state determination, countdown calculation, and progress computation in a single function body with 4 distinct return paths

campaign.ts render() computes barWidth and a lines array (lines 37-41) that are intermediary artifacts — barWidth is only used to size a fallback separator, and the lines array is the final output but goes through an unnecessary intermediate step
layout.ts findOptimalAssignment() is well-extracted as a focused function with a single responsibility (bin-packing), demonstrating good design discipline in the layout subsystem
Segment interface is clean — each segment has a focused render() and enabled() contract with no overloaded responsibilities
model.ts render() has a 3-part line2 layout section (lines 58-89) that handles gap distribution with 9+ local variables, mixing content assembly with visual spacing logic
All five segments correctly return Block objects matching their declared Segment.priority values (previously fixed: model=30, campaign=20, usage=15, context=10, git=40)

Segment.enabled() and Segment.render() contracts are honored consistently — enabled() checks are pure predicates, render() always returns a valid Block even when data is missing (e.g., campaign.ts line 11, git.ts line 38, usage.ts line 29)
getActiveCampaign() return type CampaignStatus | null accurately reflects all code paths — returns null when no campaign matches (line 136), CampaignStatus for all other branches
loadConfig() always returns StatusBlocksConfig even when the file is missing (catch branch constructs a default), matching its declared return type

campaignSegment.enabled() calls getActiveCampaign() and render() calls it again — the enabled/render double-call is consistent with the Segment interface contract but means campaign state could change between calls
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The campaign segment double-invocation of getActiveCampaign() means enabled() and render() could see different campaign states if called at a state boundary. The env var theme cast in config.ts bypasses the type contract silently.

evidence: campaign.ts computes barWidth (line 37) and constructs a lines array (lines 38-41) that is used only for width calculation (line 43) — the barWidth variable name is misleading since it is not actually a bar width but a minimum width derived from line content lengths

layout.ts line 171 applies row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5 with only a brief comment ('extra right margin for Claude Code's notification panel') but no explanation of why 5 specifically or how it relates to INK_PADDING which already accounts for Claude Code padding

engine.ts weekend progress calculation (lines 77-80) uses secsSinceFriPeak = daysSinceFri * 86400 + (currentSecs - peakEndSecs) which can go negative on Saturday before peakEndSecs hour, but is clamped by Math.max(0, ...) on line 80 — the negative intermediate value is not obviously intentional
Control flow in segments is generally clean — early returns for missing data, no dead branches, no redundant conditions
effort.ts resolveEffort() has clear cascade logic: try transcript, then try settings, with explicit null fallback

index.ts line 14: JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData — casts external stdin input without any runtime validation. If Claude Code sends malformed JSON or a different schema, the code will throw at arbitrary property access points rather than at the parse boundary

config.ts line 17: envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'] — casts an arbitrary string env var to the 'default' | 'minimal' | 'full' union type without checking if the value is actually one of those strings

effort.ts line 35: match[1]!.toLowerCase() as EffortLevel — has the same cast-without-validation pattern, but here the regex guarantees the match group is one of low|medium|high|max, making the cast provably safe
effort.ts line 49: settings.effortLevel as EffortLevel — this cast is preceded by an includes() check on line 48, making it provably safe
types.ts provides well-structured interfaces with appropriate use of optional fields (rate_limits?, cwd?, transcript_path?) that match the upstream data model
All segment render() return types match the Block interface correctly — id, priority, width, lines are always present
config.ts line 15: JSON.parse(raw) as StatusBlocksConfig — casts config file contents without validation, though this is lower severity since it is user-controlled local config

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The codebase has a clean, acyclic dependency graph with clear layering: types and utilities flow downward, segment implementations flow upward to the layout orchestrator. Hub modules (types.ts, colors.ts) are pure data/utility with no mutable state or side effects, which is the right kind of hub. The main architectural friction point is that layout.ts manually imports and registers all segments, coupling the orchestrator to every segment implementation — but for a 5-segment codebase, this is pragmatic rather than problematic.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The dependency structure is intentional and well-layered with no cycles, no shared mutable state, and no import-time side effects crossing boundaries. The hardcoded segment registration in layout.ts is the only real boundary concern, and it has low confidence as a defect given the codebase size. This places it solidly above 80 (a developer would mostly trust boundaries) but below 90 because the manual segment registration pattern creates a known friction point that would compound as the codebase grows.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has clear, domain-aligned decomposition where a new developer can immediately understand why each file and directory exists. The data flow is linear and predictable: stdin JSON goes through parsing, config loading, terminal width detection, segment rendering, and layout composition. The campaigns subsystem is well-bounded, segments are cohesive single-responsibility units, and the two entry points (CLI interactive and piped stdin) converge naturally through the layout orchestrator.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: A developer working here would find ownership boundaries clear and change localization strong — modifying a segment never ripples beyond its file, adding a campaign never touches segment code. This earns a score well above 80 (solid but uneven). The minor doc drift in cli.ts help text and the absence of a CLAUDE.md-style in-repo architecture guide for contributors keep it just below 90, but the decomposition quality is genuinely strong for a 14-file codebase.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase is highly consistent in naming conventions (camelCase throughout), export patterns (named exports everywhere), and segment behavioral protocols (all five segments follow the identical Segment interface structure). The one notable convention outlier is the campaign/promo identity split, where the segment's runtime id ('promo'), its filename (campaign.ts), and its export name (campaignSegment) use different terms for the same concept. This creates a real navigation impediment but is localized to a single file.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: A developer can see patterns in one segment and trust them in others — the structural consistency is strong. The priority mismatch that was previously flagged has been fixed. The campaign/promo naming split is a concrete convention outlier that a new developer would stumble over, pulling the score below 90. But the overall convention health is clearly above 80, with only this single isolated inconsistency rather than systemic drift.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The error strategy is fundamentally sound for a status line tool: fail gracefully by swallowing non-critical errors and degrading to simpler output. The two strategies (try-catch-swallow for I/O operations and return-null for optional data) map to distinct roles and are mostly consistent within each layer. However, the rationale documentation is uneven — effort.ts explains every catch, while sibling modules leave catches bare, forcing a developer to infer why errors are swallowed. The index.ts outer catch was previously a silent swallower but now properly writes to stderr.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error strategy is intentionally tolerant (correct for a status line tool that must never break Claude Code's UI), but the inconsistent documentation of catch block intent creates friction when a developer needs to distinguish deliberate swallowing from accidental swallowing. This is below 80 because the bare catches recur across multiple files and a developer encountering them cannot predict whether the swallowing is intentional without reading the surrounding context. The fix is straightforward — add brief rationale comments — but the current state creates enough 'why is this silent?' moments to warrant the lower score.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong naming discipline overall, with domain-specific terms used precisely and consistently across most modules. Function names like formatTokens, visibleLength, and getTimeInTz reveal intent immediately. The two open issues — the campaign/promo identity split and the resetCountdown misnomer — are real but localized, affecting navigation and readability in specific spots rather than systemically undermining the naming quality.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Names are mostly precise enough that a developer can predict behavior without reading implementations, placing this near the 90 anchor. However, the campaign/promo vocabulary divergence crosses multiple files (campaign.ts, layout.ts DEFAULT_ORDER) and would confuse a new developer searching for the promo segment by filename, pulling the score toward 80. The resetCountdown misnomer is a smaller local issue. Together these create enough friction to land at 82.5.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: Abstractions in this codebase are lean and earned. The Segment/Block interface pair provides genuine polymorphic value with 5 implementations, and every layer of indirection adds meaningful behavior. There are no pass-through wrappers, no single-implementation interfaces masquerading as flexibility, and no wide option bags threaded through layers. The single-use segment files are by design — they are pluggable units consumed by a single orchestrator.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase exemplifies the 90 anchor: a developer would trust what they see, with only minor friction. The Segment interface earns its existence through polymorphic usage, Block provides a clean boundary contract, and there is zero wrapper ceremony. The only minor concerns are unused fields in StatusBlocksConfig (separator, showCampaigns) that hint at speculative API surface, keeping the score just above 90 rather than approaching 95.

dependency_health.dimension_character: This codebase has exemplary dependency health. It uses zero production dependencies, relying entirely on Node.js built-in APIs for all functionality including date/timezone handling, file I/O, terminal detection, and ANSI rendering. The import graph is clean with no circular dependencies, and the two devDependencies (typescript, @types/node) are the absolute minimum for a TypeScript project.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is as lean as possible — zero production deps means no version conflicts, no supply chain risk, no duplicate libraries, and no heavy deps for light use. This exceeds the 90 anchor (strong with minor concerns) and approaches exemplary. The only reason not to score 100 is the mild bidirectional coupling between src/ and src/segments/ which is architecturally appropriate but technically present. A developer working here would never encounter dependency friction.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase shows uneven low-level craft. Simple segments (context, git, usage, campaign) are concise and direct with clear variable names and minimal branching. However, the more complex modules — model.ts gap distribution, engine.ts state machine, and layout.ts optimizer — contain dense arithmetic with many local variables and deeply nested control flow that require significant mental effort to trace. Two of three previously flagged issues have been resolved (layout.ts extraction, campaign.ts dead code), but model.ts gap arithmetic and engine.ts multi-concern mixing persist.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits at the 80 anchor: a developer regularly encounters local complexity that makes individual functions harder to follow than they should be. The model.ts gap distribution (12+ variables for whitespace arithmetic) and engine.ts's 88-line multi-concern function are the primary drags. These are offset by the clean, simple segment implementations and the successful refactoring of layout.ts. The uneven quality — some modules exemplary, others requiring careful reading — places this solidly at 78.5.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Module handoffs are generally clean and compositional. The Segment interface creates an effective plugin-style boundary where layout.ts orchestrates segments without knowledge of their internals, and data translation happens at well-defined points (config.ts for config, engine.ts for campaign state, effort.ts for effort resolution). The one recurring friction point is layout.ts embedding display-policy constants (row1 width reduction, INK_PADDING) directly in orchestration logic rather than isolating them as named configuration, creating a seam where policy and mechanics are tangled.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The compositional segment architecture and linear data flow push this toward 85+, but the hardcoded row1 width reduction (maxRowWidth - 5) mixes display policy into orchestration without explanation or naming, and the overall seam count is small enough that this single issue represents a meaningful fraction of boundary design. This places the codebase around 78 — mostly good seams with notable friction at the layout policy boundary.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The codebase has zero automated test coverage — no test framework, no test files, no test script. Every critical path is unvalidated: the ANSI string utilities relied upon by all 5 segments, the timezone-aware campaign engine with weekend/peak/off-peak state machine, the combinatorial layout optimizer, and the effort resolution with JSONL parsing. A developer making changes has no safety net and must rely entirely on manual visual verification via the preview command.
test_strategy.score_rationale: A score of 5 reflects a codebase where no test infrastructure exists at all. The global anchor for 60 describes 'meaningful risk goes unvalidated' — this codebase is far below that because there is literally nothing to validate against. The pure-function utilities (colors.ts, engine.ts helpers) would be trivial to test and provide high value, making the absence more notable. The only thing preventing a score of 0 is that the code is structured in a way that would be easy to test (pure functions, typed interfaces, minimal side effects in core logic).

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The API surface is compact and consistent. The Segment interface provides a uniform shape across all 5 implementations, all exports are synchronous, parameter counts are low, and the two previously reported coherence issues (priority mismatch, dead export) have been fixed. The remaining friction is minor: the allocWidth parameter in render() is only meaningful to one of five segments, creating slight contract dishonesty at the interface level, though this does not cause practical confusion because unused parameters are simply ignored.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores well because a developer can predict API shape and behavior from seeing one segment example — the Segment interface, colors.ts utilities, and config/campaign APIs all follow consistent patterns. The fixed priority and export issues demonstrate active improvement. The allocWidth underuse is minor enough (a single unused parameter) that it warrants a note but not a significant score reduction. This places the score around 88 — strong with minor rough edges.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization consistency is not applicable to this codebase. claude-statusblocks is a local CLI tool that reads stdin JSON and writes formatted terminal output. It has no HTTP endpoints, no user authentication, no database access, no service-to-service communication, and no protected resources. All file access is to local user configuration files under standard OS permissions. The authorization dimension has zero attack surface in this architecture.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: A score of 100 is appropriate because the dimension's look-for items (route handler auth gaps, RLS inconsistency, permission magic literals, mixed trust boundaries, admin bypasses) are entirely absent from this codebase's architecture. There are no auth patterns to be inconsistent about. A developer working here would never encounter authorization-related friction because the tool operates entirely within the local user's context with no network or multi-user concerns.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase shows very little AI-generated debt. Code is concise and purpose-driven with a low comment ratio (5.7%), no restating comments, no debug logging in production paths, and no pass-through wrappers. The previously flagged dead code in campaign.ts has been cleaned up. The only mild signal is cli.ts using raw ANSI escape sequences for output formatting instead of the project's own color() utility, which gives it a slightly template-like quality.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A developer's attention would be spent on logic, not noise — placing this near the 90 anchor for 'mostly clean with small pockets of boilerplate.' The cli.ts inline ANSI pattern is a minor departure from idiomatic project conventions but does not meaningfully impede understanding. The stale-code fix in campaign.ts eliminates the previously most significant AI-debt signal.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase shows no meaningful migration debt. It is uniformly TypeScript with ESM imports, has no deprecated symbols or compatibility shims, and no stale migration TODOs. The rate_limits migration from external API to native JSON field has been completed cleanly with no residual dual-path code. The only detectable signal is one var declaration among 13 let/const usages, which is trivially minor.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: A developer would never encounter old-vs-new pattern confusion or need to decide which approach to follow — placing this above the 90 anchor for 'mostly complete with minor legacy residue.' The single var-vs-let/const signal is the only detectable residue and carries negligible cognitive cost. The completed rate_limits migration demonstrates that migrations are finished cleanly rather than left partial.

package_organization.dimension_character: The codebase has a clean three-directory layout that maps well to its domain responsibilities. Each directory groups related files with coherent fan-in/fan-out profiles. The 14-file, 971-LOC size is well below the threshold where organizational complexity becomes necessary, and the current structure serves navigability well. Root-level files like types.ts and colors.ts earn their placement through high cross-directory fan-in.
package_organization.score_rationale: A developer can predict where to find things based on directory structure alone, placing this above the 90 anchor. The three directories have clear purpose, directory names match contained responsibilities, and no straggler roots or misplaced files exist. The bidirectional src/ to segments/ coupling is structurally inherent to the design (segments need types, layout needs segments) and appropriate for this codebase size.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase is mostly clean on initialization coupling — no import-time side effects, no circular dependencies, and no boot-order sensitivity. Two module-level path constants (CONFIG_PATH, SETTINGS_PATH) bake homedir() at import time, creating stale snapshots that cannot be overridden for testing. This stands in contrast to effort.ts which correctly resolves homedir() at call time. Module-level caches (gitCache, effort cache) use TTL invalidation and are pragmatic for this use case.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The import-time homedir() baking in config.ts and cli.ts creates real testability friction — a developer cannot mock or override the home directory path after module import, which would matter for unit testing loadConfig() or init(). This pulls the score toward the 80 anchor where 'a developer encounters import-time side effects or environment reads at module scope that create fragility.' The existence of the correct pattern in effort.ts makes this an inconsistency rather than a systemic problem, keeping it near 80 rather than below.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase generally follows good design principles — the Segment interface is clean, layout logic is well-extracted into focused helpers, and most files have a single coherent responsibility. However, two hotspots drag it down: getActiveCampaign() tangles multiple distinct responsibilities (time resolution, state classification, progress calculation) in one body, and model.ts render() mixes content generation with visual spacing arithmetic through excessive local variable proliferation.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The segment architecture and layout extraction show solid design instincts, pushing toward 80+. However, the getActiveCampaign() multi-responsibility body and model.ts gap distribution complexity are genuine design issues where a developer would struggle to modify one concern without understanding the others. These pull the score into the mid-70s — a developer would mostly find focused functions but would hit two clear cases where responsibilities are tangled.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong contract discipline. Return types match all code paths, the Segment interface is honored uniformly across all five implementations, and fallback behaviors are explicit (null returns, default configs). The previously reported priority mismatch has been fixed. The remaining contract gaps are minor: a potential TOCTOU between enabled() and render() in the campaign segment, and the env var theme cast that bypasses type validation.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: A developer can trust that functions do what their signatures promise — return types are accurate, the Segment interface is consistently implemented, and error paths return valid fallback values rather than undefined. The strong contract discipline across the codebase pushes toward 90. The campaign enabled/render TOCTOU and unvalidated theme cast are real but low-severity gaps that prevent a higher score.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Most control flow in the codebase is direct and traceable — segments use early returns for missing data, the layout optimizer has clear termination conditions, and effort resolution follows a readable cascade. However, two open issues reduce clarity: the campaign segment's intermediate barWidth/lines computation that serves only as an indirect width calculation, and the unexplained 5-char row 1 offset in layout.ts that sits alongside INK_PADDING without documenting their relationship. The weekend progress arithmetic in engine.ts also has a non-obvious negative intermediate that relies on a downstream clamp.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: A developer would find most logic paths clear and predictable, but would be confused by the campaign.ts barWidth/lines indirection (why compute a bar width that is not a bar?), the magic 5 in layout.ts (how does this relate to INK_PADDING?), and the engine.ts negative-intermediate progress math. These are concentrated in a few files but represent real comprehension barriers. The overall score lands at 78 — solid but with recurring spots where intent is obscured.

type_safety.dimension_character: The codebase has well-structured type definitions — StatusLineData, Block, Segment, Campaign, and StatusBlocksConfig interfaces accurately describe the data shapes, and internal code consistently uses these types correctly. However, at every external boundary (stdin JSON, config file, environment variables), the code uses 'as' casts without runtime validation. The effort.ts module shows the right pattern — validating before casting — but this discipline is not applied at the two most critical boundaries: stdin parsing and env var theme resolution.
type_safety.score_rationale: Internal type usage is solid — return types match, interfaces are well-designed, and optional fields correctly model nullable upstream data. This would push toward 85+. However, the two open type-safety issues at critical boundaries (stdin cast and theme env var cast) represent real gaps where runtime behavior can diverge from declared types. The stdin cast is particularly impactful since it is the primary entry point for all data. These boundary gaps pull the score to the mid-70s — a developer would need to be aware that type annotations at I/O boundaries do not reflect runtime guarantees.

issues.summary: layout.ts hardcodes all segment imports — adding a segment requires editing layout.ts
issues.suggestion: For a 5-segment codebase this is pragmatic and not worth abstracting. If segment count grows beyond ~8, consider a segments/index.ts barrel that exports the array, keeping the registration concern in the segments directory. For now, add a comment noting the manual registration pattern.
evidence: Lines 4-8 of layout.ts import each segment individually; line 10 manually constructs ALL_SEGMENTS array
Adding a new segment requires: creating the segment file, adding import to layout.ts, adding to ALL_SEGMENTS array, and adding to DEFAULT_ORDER

issues.summary: CLI help text lists only 3 of 5 segments, omitting 'promo' and 'usage'
issues.suggestion: Add the missing segments to the Blocks section in help(): 'promo' (promotional rate status with peak/off-peak countdown) and 'usage' (5-hour and 7-day rate limit bars). Keep the same format as existing entries.
evidence: Lines 69-71 of cli.ts help() list only context, model, git under Blocks section
The actual DEFAULT_ORDER in layout.ts includes 'context', 'model', 'promo', 'git', 'usage' — 5 segments
Users reading help output would not know promo and usage segments exist or can be configured

issues.summary: Campaign segment uses id 'promo' while file is campaign.ts and export is campaignSegment
issues.suggestion: Either rename the file to promo.ts and export promoSegment to match the id, or change the id to 'campaign' and update DEFAULT_ORDER in layout.ts. Since the segment renders promotional rate status and the CLAUDE.md refers to it as 'promo', renaming file/export to promo.ts/promoSegment is the lower-friction path.
evidence: campaign.ts line 6: id is 'promo'
campaign.ts line 5: export is named campaignSegment
layout.ts line 11: DEFAULT_ORDER references 'promo'

All other segments have consistent naming: context.ts exports contextSegment with id 'context', model.ts exports modelSegment with id 'model', git.ts exports gitSegment with id 'git', usage.ts exports usageSegment with id 'usage'

issues.summary: Catch blocks inconsistently document their rationale — some have comments, others are bare
issues.suggestion: Add brief rationale comments to the undocumented catches: index.ts inner catches should note 'TTY detection fallback' and 'tput fallback'; config.ts should note 'missing or malformed config file — use defaults'; git.ts should note 'not a git repo or git not available'. Follow the pattern already established in effort.ts.
evidence: effort.ts documents all 3 catches: /* skip malformed lines */, /* file not readable */, /* no settings */
index.ts line 28: catch { /* */ } — empty comment placeholder for TTY detection failure
index.ts line 33: catch { /* */ } — empty comment placeholder for tput cols failure
config.ts line 19: catch { — bare, swallows config file read/parse failure
git.ts line 26: catch { return null; } — bare, swallows all git command failures

issues.summary: campaignSegment export uses id 'promo' creating name/identity mismatch across codebase
issues.suggestion: Rename file to promo.ts and export as promoSegment to match the id, since 'promo' accurately describes the segment's purpose (promotional rate display). Update the import in layout.ts accordingly.
evidence: campaign.ts exports campaignSegment with id: 'promo' (line 6)
layout.ts DEFAULT_ORDER references 'promo' (line 11) but imports campaignSegment from campaign.ts (line 7)
A developer searching for 'campaign' would find the file but miss the config reference; searching for 'promo' would find the config but miss the file

issues.summary: resetCountdown sounds like an action (reset the countdown) rather than a formatter
issues.suggestion: Rename to formatResetCountdown to match the codebase's format* naming convention and clarify it is a formatter, not an action.
evidence: resetCountdown (line 4) is a pure formatter: takes an epoch timestamp, returns a human-readable countdown string like '2h30m'
The codebase has established format* as the convention for formatters: formatTokens in context.ts (line 4), formatCountdown in engine.ts (line 41)
The 'reset' prefix suggests an imperative mutation, but the function is side-effect-free

issues.summary: model.ts 3-part line2 layout uses 12+ local variables for manual gap distribution
issues.suggestion: Extract the 3-part gap distribution into a helper function like spreadParts(parts: string[], targetWidth: number, separator?: string): string that encapsulates the dot-vs-space decision and gap arithmetic. This reduces model.ts render() to calling spreadParts(parts, line1Width) and makes the layout logic testable in isolation.
evidence: Lines 58-81: the 3-part layout branches on spareWithDots <= 12 to decide between dot-separated and space-separated layouts
The dot-separated branch (lines 67-75) introduces clamped, g, extra, g1, g2, g3, g4 — seven variables for distributing whitespace evenly across four gaps
A developer reading this must mentally simulate the arithmetic to understand what the output looks like, as the variable names (g1-g4) reveal nothing about which gap they represent

issues.summary: getActiveCampaign() mixes time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation in 88 lines
issues.suggestion: Extract the weekend timing calculation (lines 70-88) into a helper like computeWeekendTiming(currentSecs, peakStartSecs, peakEndSecs, dow) returning {secsUntilChange, progress}. Similarly extract the off-peak weekday timing (lines 104-131). This keeps getActiveCampaign focused on state classification while delegating arithmetic to focused helpers.
evidence: Lines 49-137: the function handles 5 distinct states (upcoming, ended, weekend, peak, off-peak) with different progress and countdown calculations for each
Weekend progress calculation (lines 72-80) uses 5 intermediate variables (dowNum, daysUntilMon, secsUntilPeak, totalWeekendSecs, daysSinceFri, secsSinceFriPeak) with non-obvious arithmetic
Off-peak weekday progress (lines 116-123) has its own set of intermediate variables for a different calculation, making it hard to see what progress means consistently across states

issues.summary: Layout embeds hardcoded row1 width reduction (maxRowWidth - 5) mixing policy with orchestration
issues.suggestion: Extract the row1 extra margin as a named constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_MARGIN = 5) next to INK_PADDING on line 12 with a comment explaining why row 1 needs additional reduction beyond the general INK_PADDING. This keeps the policy declaration together with its sibling constant.
evidence: Line 171: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5 — the magic number 5 represents a notification panel margin but is unnamed and undocumented alongside INK_PADDING which is properly named and commented on line 12
This constant is passed into findOptimalAssignment as a parameter but its rationale is only recoverable by reading Claude Code's UI behavior externally

issues.summary: No test framework or test files exist anywhere in the codebase
issues.suggestion: Add vitest as a devDependency (lightweight, ESM-native, TypeScript out-of-box) and a 'test' script in package.json. Start with unit tests for the highest-value pure-logic modules: colors.ts (visibleLength, padRight, truncate), campaigns/engine.ts (getActiveCampaign with vi.useFakeTimers), and layout.ts (findOptimalAssignment with known block widths).
evidence: package.json devDependencies has only typescript and @types/node — no test runner
No test script defined in package.json scripts
No *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, or __tests__/ directories found in repository

issues.summary: Campaign engine's timezone-aware date arithmetic has zero test coverage
issues.suggestion: Write unit tests for getActiveCampaign() using vi.useFakeTimers() to pin Date.now() and verify each state transition: upcoming (before campaign start), active-boosted (off-peak weekday), active-normal (peak hours), weekend, and ended (after campaign end). Test countdown formatting and progress values at boundary times.
evidence: getActiveCampaign() contains 88 lines of date arithmetic with timezone conversion via Intl.DateTimeFormat, weekend detection, peak/off-peak classification, countdown calculation, and progress computation
getTimeInTz() uses formatToParts which has edge cases (hour=24 normalization on line 27)
Weekend countdown calculates days-until-Monday with manual arithmetic (line 73: daysUntilMon = 8 - dowNum)
Off-peak progress calculation after peak hours involves cross-day arithmetic (line 120: 86400 - peakEndSecs + currentSecs)

issues.summary: Most-imported utility (colors.ts, 6 importers) has no tests for ANSI-aware string operations
issues.suggestion: Add unit tests for visibleLength (plain text, styled text, nested styles, empty string), padRight (shorter/exact/longer inputs with ANSI), and truncate (cut point inside ANSI sequence, zero width, multi-style strings, string with trailing reset codes).
evidence: visibleLength() strips ANSI codes via regex — incorrect stripping would cascade errors to all 5 segments and layout.ts
padRight() delegates to truncate() for over-length strings — ANSI-aware truncation has edge cases (cut point inside escape sequence)
truncate() manually walks the string tracking visible characters and skipping ANSI sequences — off-by-one errors would be invisible without tests

issues.summary: Combinatorial layout row-assignment algorithm has no test coverage
issues.suggestion: Test findOptimalAssignment() with known block widths and verify: single block returns [0], two blocks fitting one row return single-row assignment, blocks forcing two rows produce correct assignment, row1 width constraint causes narrower blocks to land on row 1. Test materializeAssignment() for correct sorting.
evidence: findOptimalAssignment() uses brute-force enumeration (numRows^n assignments) with pyramid validation and scoring — complex combinatorial logic with no tests
The algorithm has subtle constraints: row1 gets a different max width, rows are sorted by width ascending, all row indices must be used
materializeAssignment() sorts groups by width — ordering affects visual output

issues.summary: Segment render() allocWidth parameter is only used by 1 of 5 implementations
issues.suggestion: This is a pragmatic interface design for a 5-segment codebase — making allocWidth optional (availWidth?: number) would add ceremony without value. No code change needed, but if more segments are added, consider whether allocWidth should be part of the interface or passed only to segments that need it via a different mechanism.
evidence: Segment interface declares render(data: StatusLineData, availWidth: number): Block
model.ts uses allocWidth to compute dirBudget for directory name truncation (line 34)
context.ts, campaign.ts, git.ts, and usage.ts all ignore the allocWidth parameter entirely — their widths are determined by content
layout.ts computes allocWidth = Math.max(20, maxRowWidth - BOX_CHROME) and passes it to all segments uniformly

issues.summary: cli.ts uses raw ANSI escape strings instead of the project's own color() utility
issues.suggestion: Import { color, c } from './colors.js' and replace raw ANSI sequences with color() calls. For example, change console.log('\x1b[1mclaude-statusblocks\x1b[0m preview') to console.log(color('claude-statusblocks', c.bold) + ' preview'). This aligns cli.ts with the project's established color infrastructure.
evidence: cli.ts lines 30, 33-34, 42, 49-51, 61 use raw '\x1b[' escape sequences for coloring, while every other file in the project uses the color()/c.* API from colors.ts
This pattern is consistent with template-pasted code rather than code written with project context

issues.summary: CONFIG_PATH and SETTINGS_PATH bake homedir() at import time, creating untestable stale snapshots
issues.suggestion: Move CONFIG_PATH computation inside loadConfig() in config.ts: replace the module-level const with a local const inside the function body. Similarly, move SETTINGS_PATH computation inside init() in cli.ts. This follows the pattern already established in effort.ts where homedir() is resolved at call time, enabling tests to mock os.homedir() or set environment variables that affect path resolution.
evidence: config.ts line 6: const CONFIG_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude-statusblocks.json') runs at import time
cli.ts line 10: const SETTINGS_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude', 'settings.json') runs at import time
In contrast, effort.ts line 46 correctly resolves homedir() inside the function body: const configDir = process.env.CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR || join(homedir(), '.claude')

issues.summary: getActiveCampaign() mixes time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation in one 88-line body
issues.suggestion: Extract a computeTimingState(campaign, hour, minute, second, dow) helper that returns {state, secsUntilChange, progress} by encapsulating the peak/off-peak/weekend classification and countdown math. getActiveCampaign() would then focus on campaign selection (date range check) and delegating to the timing helper for the active campaign.
evidence: Lines 49-137: function handles timezone resolution (line 64), weekend detection and countdown (lines 70-88), peak detection and countdown (lines 90-102), off-peak countdown and progress (lines 104-131), and the no-peak-hours fallback (line 133) — five distinct concerns in one body

The weekend branch (lines 70-88) contains its own progress calculation with totalWeekendSecs/secsSinceFriPeak, while the off-peak branch (lines 116-123) has a separate totalOffPeak/secsSinceLastPeak calculation — these are parallel concerns that could share a structure

issues.summary: model.ts 3-part line2 layout uses 9+ local variables for manual gap distribution across effort/duration/version
issues.suggestion: Extract a function like spreadParts(parts: string[], targetWidth: number, separator?: string): string that handles distributing N parts across a target width with optional separators. This replaces the manual variable cascade with a reusable, focused layout helper.
evidence: Lines 58-81: the 3-part branch creates dot, dotLen, eLen, dLen, vLen, contentWidth, spareWithDots, clamped, g, extra, g1, g2, g3, g4, remaining, leftGap, rightGap — 17 local variables for spacing three strings across a width
The two sub-branches (dots vs plain spacing) each construct line2 differently, mixing visual formatting decisions with arithmetic

issues.summary: High cyclomatic complexity in engine.ts driven by interleaved state classification and arithmetic
issues.suggestion: The cyclomatic complexity is a symptom of the mixed responsibilities identified in engine_mixed_responsibilities. Extracting computeTimingState would naturally reduce the branch depth in getActiveCampaign().
evidence: Mechanical signal: smells detector flagged 1x High cyclomatic complexity (>15 branches)
The complexity stems from nested if/else branches for weekend, peak, off-peak-before-peak, and off-peak-after-peak — each with its own countdown and progress calculations

issues.summary: campaignSegment.enabled() and render() both call getActiveCampaign() independently, risking state divergence
issues.suggestion: This is low-severity because the render() null guard (line 11) handles the case safely. However, the contract would be tighter if campaign state were resolved once. One approach: cache the result in a module-level variable during enabled() and reuse it in render(), similar to gitSegment's cache pattern.
evidence: Line 8: enabled() calls getActiveCampaign() to check !== null
Line 10: render() calls getActiveCampaign() again independently
If the campaign ends between the enabled() call and the render() call (e.g., at midnight boundary), enabled() returns true but render() gets null and returns a zero-width block

issues.summary: campaign.ts render() computes barWidth variable that misleadingly suggests a progress bar but is only used for fallback separator sizing
issues.suggestion: Rename barWidth to contentWidth or maxLineWidth to reflect its actual purpose — it computes the wider of the two content lines for separator sizing. This removes the misleading bar reference.
evidence: Line 37: const barWidth = Math.max(visibleLength(line1), visibleLength(line2)) — named 'barWidth' but no bar is rendered
Line 40: barWidth is only used in line2 fallback: line2 || color('─'.repeat(barWidth), c.gray) — it sizes a horizontal rule, not a bar
The name 'barWidth' is a vestige of removed progress bar logic (previously flagged as dead code)

issues.summary: layout.ts applies an additional unexplained 5-char offset for row 1 beyond the existing INK_PADDING
issues.suggestion: Extract the 5 as a named constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_OVERLAP = 5) with a comment explaining that Claude Code's notification badge overlaps only the first row's right edge by approximately 5 characters. This makes the relationship between INK_PADDING (general) and NOTIFICATION_OVERLAP (row-1-specific) explicit.
evidence: Line 12: const INK_PADDING = 4; // Claude Code's outer paddingX: 2 on each side
Line 157: const maxRowWidth = Math.max(40, termWidth - INK_PADDING);
Line 171: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5; — subtracts an additional 5 with only 'extra right margin for notification panel' comment
No documentation explains how 5 was derived or why the notification panel only affects row 1

issues.summary: Weekend progress calculation produces a negative intermediate value before clamping
issues.suggestion: Add a brief comment above line 79 explaining that secsSinceFriPeak measures seconds elapsed since Friday's peak ended, and that the clamp on line 80 handles the edge case where Saturday has not yet reached the peak-end hour. This makes the formula's intent explicit.
evidence: Line 79: secsSinceFriPeak = daysSinceFri * 86400 + (currentSecs - peakEndSecs)
On Saturday at 6 AM with peakEndSecs=50400 (14:00): secsSinceFriPeak = 1 * 86400 + (21600 - 50400) = 86400 - 28800 = 57600 — this happens to be positive
But the formula is structurally relying on daysSinceFri * 86400 always dominating the (currentSecs - peakEndSecs) term, which is true but non-obvious
Line 80: Math.max(0, Math.min(1, ...)) provides a safety clamp but the intent of the arithmetic is unclear without it

issues.summary: JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData casts external stdin input without runtime validation
issues.suggestion: Add minimal runtime guards after parsing: if (!data?.model?.display_name || !data?.context_window || !data?.workspace?.current_dir) throw new Error('Invalid statusline data: missing required fields'). This validates the most-accessed fields at the boundary without adding a schema validation dependency.
evidence: Line 14: const data = JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData — the 'as' cast tells TypeScript to trust that the parsed JSON matches StatusLineData, but there is no runtime check

If upstream sends {model: null} or omits context_window, the code will throw at an arbitrary downstream access point (e.g., data.context_window.used_percentage in context.ts line 15) rather than at the parse boundary
The outer try/catch (line 38) catches these errors but produces a generic error message that does not indicate which field was missing or malformed

issues.summary: Environment variable cast to theme union type without validation
issues.suggestion: Add validation before the cast: const validThemes: StatusBlocksConfig['theme'][] = ['default', 'minimal', 'full']; if (envTheme && validThemes.includes(envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'])) config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme']. This follows the pattern already established in effort.ts.
evidence: Line 17: config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'] — casts arbitrary string to 'default' | 'minimal' | 'full' without checking
Line 22: same pattern repeated in the catch branch
effort.ts lines 48-49 shows the correct pattern: validates with includes() before casting

add.description: Dependencies flow cleanly: types.ts and colors.ts are shared foundations, segments depend on them, layout.ts orchestrates segments. No cycles exist despite bidirectional directory-level coupling (src/ <-> src/segments/).

The two most-imported modules (types.ts at 11 importers, colors.ts at 6) contain only type definitions and pure functions respectively — no mutable state, no side effects. This is the ideal hub pattern.

add.header: No shared mutable state across module boundaries
add.description: Module-level caches (gitCache in git.ts, cache in effort.ts) are local to their modules and not exported. No cross-module coordination through globals.
layout.ts is the sole consumer of segment implementations, keeping the segment-to-orchestrator boundary clean with a single integration surface.

Each directory maps to a domain capability: segments/ for rendering units, campaigns/ for promotional logic, root src/ for orchestration and shared utilities. A new developer can predict file locations from domain knowledge alone.

Data flows strictly stdin -> parse -> config -> detect width -> render segments -> layout -> stdout. No callbacks, no event systems, no bidirectional orchestration. This makes the system easy to reason about end-to-end.
The campaigns/ directory fully encapsulates promotional rate logic with data.ts for definitions and engine.ts for evaluation. Only the campaign segment consumes it, keeping the boundary clean.
Each segment owns its rendering and data interpretation. Modifying a segment's output never ripples to other segments or the layout engine (beyond width calculations that are inherently coupled).

remove: campaign.ts has dead progress bar logic and contradictory inline comments from iterative development

add.description: All functions, variables, and exports use camelCase across all directories (campaigns/, src/, segments/). No snake_case drift or mixed conventions.

All five segments implement the identical Segment interface shape with consistent patterns: named export of const xyzSegment, matching id in Block return, same enabled/render contract. A developer can predict the structure of any segment from seeing one.
All modules across src/ and src/segments/ use named exports exclusively — no default exports, no class exports. This makes import autocomplete predictable.
Previously, 3 of 5 segments returned Block.priority values differing from their Segment.priority declarations. This has been fixed — all segments now return consistent priority values.

remove: 3 of 5 segments return Block.priority values that differ from their Segment.priority definitions

add.description: A status line tool must never crash or produce error output that breaks Claude Code's Ink renderer. The catch-and-swallow / return-null strategy is deliberately tolerant — correct for this domain where showing less is better than crashing.

I/O-dependent operations (terminal detection, config loading, transcript parsing) use try-catch-swallow with fallback values. Optional data providers (git info, campaign status) return null to signal unavailability. These map cleanly to their roles.

add.header: Index.ts outer catch now writes stderr diagnostics
add.description: Previously flagged as silently swallowing all errors, the top-level catch now writes [claude-statusblocks] error messages to stderr while still outputting a blank line to stdout for graceful degradation.

add.header: Effort.ts catch documentation sets good precedent
add.description: effort.ts documents every catch block with brief inline comments explaining why the error is swallowed. This establishes a clear convention that other modules do not yet follow.

remove: Top-level catch in index.ts silently swallows all errors, outputting blank line with no diagnostics

add.description: Type names (StatusLineData, Block, Segment, Campaign, CampaignStatus) map directly to domain concepts. A developer can navigate the type system without needing to translate from generic names to domain meaning.
All five segments follow the {domain}Segment naming pattern (contextSegment, modelSegment, gitSegment, usageSegment, campaignSegment), making the segment registry immediately scannable.
Time-related variables consistently include units in their names (peakStartSecs, currentSecs, secsUntilPeak, total_duration_ms), preventing unit confusion in arithmetic.
Functions that convert raw values to display strings use the format prefix (formatTokens, formatCountdown), establishing a recognizable convention for side-effect-free transformations.

The Segment interface has 5 concrete implementations all consumed polymorphically via ALL_SEGMENTS array in layout.ts. This is genuine abstraction leverage — adding a new segment requires only implementing the interface and registering it.

Block serves as the boundary type between segment rendering and layout orchestration. Each segment produces a Block; layout consumes Blocks without knowing segment internals. This keeps the two concerns decoupled without ceremony.
Every function in the codebase adds behavior, validation, or transformation. There are no delegation-only wrappers that forward args without adding value, keeping the indirection cost at near-zero.

add.header: Single-use segments are by architectural design
add.description: Each segment file is imported only by layout.ts, which is the intended plugin-consumer architecture. Single-use here is not a code smell but the natural result of a registry pattern with a single orchestrator.

The project deliberately avoids external runtime dependencies, using Node.js built-in modules (fs, path, os, child_process, Intl.DateTimeFormat) for all functionality. This eliminates supply chain risk and version conflict concerns entirely.

add.header: Native Intl API for timezone handling
add.description: Timezone-aware date arithmetic uses Intl.DateTimeFormat with formatToParts rather than pulling in moment/dayjs/luxon. This is the right tradeoff for a CLI tool that only needs hour-level timezone resolution.
Only typescript and @types/node as devDependencies — the absolute minimum for a TypeScript Node.js project with no test framework, linter, or bundler overhead.

add.header: Simple segments are concise and direct
add.description: Segment render functions for context, git, usage, and campaign are each under 35 lines with clear variable names and minimal branching. They build 2-line outputs with straightforward string assembly, making them easy to read and modify.

findOptimalAssignment and materializeAssignment were extracted from render(), reducing the orchestrator to 24 lines. This is a successful application of balanced extraction that keeps concerns separated without micro-fragmentation.

add.header: colors.ts truncate uses efficient ANSI-aware walk
add.description: The truncate function uses character-by-character pointer advancement with ANSI sequence skipping, which is the simplest correct approach for this problem. Despite being low-level, the logic is intention-revealing.

add.header: Campaign dead code and stale comments removed
add.description: Previously flagged dead bar variable, unused lines array, and stale iteration comments in campaign.ts have been cleaned up. The render function now builds output directly without intermediate dead computation.

The Segment interface (id, priority, enabled, render) creates a uniform contract that lets layout.ts orchestrate all segments identically. No per-segment conditionals or special-case handoffs exist in the orchestrator — each segment is a black box returning a Block.

add.header: Linear data flow from stdin to stdout
add.description: Data flows in one direction: index.ts parses JSON, calls render() with (data, termWidth, config), layout.ts maps segments to blocks and rows, then outputs lines. No bidirectional callbacks or tangled coordination between modules.

All config loading, env var override, and JSON parsing is contained in config.ts:loadConfig(). Consumers receive a typed StatusBlocksConfig with no need to handle raw env vars or file I/O at their boundaries.

add.header: Effort resolution encapsulated behind clean boundary
add.description: effort.ts:resolveEffort() accepts a transcript path and returns an EffortLevel, encapsulating its own caching, multi-source fallback, and JSONL parsing. The model segment consumes the result without knowledge of resolution mechanics.

add.header: Preview command provides manual visual verification
add.description: The 'preview' CLI command renders at 120/80/50 column widths with mock data, providing a visual smoke test. This is the only verification mechanism but is not automated and cannot catch regressions.

add.header: Code structure is test-friendly despite no tests
add.description: Core logic is organized as pure functions (visibleLength, formatTokens, formatCountdown, findOptimalAssignment) with typed interfaces (Segment, Block). Side effects are isolated to index.ts and git.ts. This structure would make test introduction straightforward.

add.header: Uniform Segment interface across all implementations
add.description: All 5 segments implement the identical Segment interface (id, priority, enabled, render) with consistent return types. A developer seeing one segment can predict the shape of all others.
Every exported function across the codebase is synchronous — no mixed sync/async patterns exist. This eliminates a common source of API surface inconsistency.

add.header: Previously reported priority and export issues fixed
add.description: Block.priority values now match Segment.priority declarations in all segments, and truncate() is no longer exported from colors.ts. Both fixes improve API surface consistency.

add.header: Low parameter counts across all public functions
add.description: No public function exceeds 3 parameters. render() takes (data, termWidth, config), segment render takes (data, allocWidth), loadConfig() takes none. This keeps the API surface easy to predict.

add.header: Local CLI tool with no auth surface
add.description: This is a stdin-to-stdout CLI renderer with no HTTP endpoints, no database, no network calls, and no multi-user concerns. Authorization patterns are not applicable to this architecture.

All 14 production files maintain a low 5.7% comment ratio with comments that explain WHY (business rules, external constraints) rather than restating code. No restating comments, no debug logging in production paths, no docstring bloat on trivial functions.

Functions use domain terms (formatTokens, tildeDir, getGitInfo, miniBar, resetCountdown) rather than generic AI-hallmark names (handleData, processItem). This indicates code written with project context rather than from a generic template.
Every function adds logic or transformation — no functions exist that simply forward arguments to another function without added behavior. The wrapper_rate in scan evidence confirms 0.0%.

add.header: cli.ts uses raw ANSI instead of color API
add.description: The CLI module inlines raw escape sequences for terminal coloring rather than importing the project's own color()/c.* infrastructure from colors.ts. This is a mild template-paste pattern but confined to one non-production-path file.

add.header: Uniform TypeScript ESM with no JS residue
add.description: All 14 source files are .ts with ESM imports using .js extensions and no require() calls. The 'type: module' package.json setting is consistently honored. No mixed JS/TS modules exist.

The migration from external API fetch to native rate_limits field in statusline JSON (commit e4e7ddf) was completed without leaving compatibility shims or dual-path code. Usage segment reads directly from data.rate_limits with no fallback to an old approach.

add.header: No deprecated symbols or stale migration TODOs
add.description: No @deprecated markers, DEPRECATED comments, or TODOs referencing migration/legacy/remove-after exist in the codebase. Active code does not call any deprecated symbols.

add.header: Three-directory layout maps to domain concerns
add.description: src/ holds core infrastructure (types, colors, config, layout, entry points), src/segments/ holds the five rendering units, and src/campaigns/ holds promotional rate data and evaluation logic. Each directory has a coherent purpose that a developer can predict.

add.header: Root files earn placement through high fan-in
add.description: types.ts (11 importers across all directories) and colors.ts (6 importers across segments and core) are genuinely cross-cutting infrastructure. Moving them into subdirectories would add import path noise without improving navigability.

At 14 files and 971 LOC, the codebase is well under the 20-file threshold where deeper organizational hierarchy becomes necessary. The current three-directory structure is proportionate to codebase size.

add.header: No import-time side effects in production paths
add.description: No module-scope code performs file I/O, network calls, or database connections at import time. Module initialization is limited to constant declarations and object literals, keeping imports side-effect-free.

add.header: Acyclic import graph with no boot-order fragility
add.description: The import graph flows cleanly: segments depend on src/ (types, colors), campaigns depend on src/ types, and src/ layout imports segments for registration. No circular dependencies or conditional/lazy imports exist.

add.header: Pragmatic module-level caches with TTL invalidation
add.description: gitCache (git.ts) and effort cache (effort.ts) are module-level mutable singletons, but their 5-second TTL invalidation and single-consumer usage patterns make them pragmatic choices for a CLI status line rather than problematic global state.

add.header: Inconsistent homedir() resolution timing across modules
add.description: config.ts and cli.ts compute path constants from homedir() at import time (creating stale snapshots), while effort.ts correctly resolves homedir() inside its function body at call time. This inconsistency means the project has two patterns for the same concern.

add.header: Clean Segment interface with focused contracts
add.description: The Segment interface (id, priority, enabled, render) keeps each segment focused on its own rendering concern. No segment mixes concerns across domain boundaries — each owns its data fetching and presentation.
findOptimalAssignment and materializeAssignment in layout.ts are cleanly separated from rendering, giving the bin-packing algorithm a focused single responsibility with clear inputs and outputs.

getActiveCampaign() mixes campaign selection, timezone resolution, state classification, and progress calculation. This makes modifying any single timing concern risky because the logic is interleaved rather than composed.

add.header: Segment return types match all code paths
add.description: Every segment's render() returns a valid Block on all paths including error/missing-data fallbacks (e.g., campaign returns {width:0, lines:['']}, git returns {width:0, lines:['']}). No undefined or missing returns.

add.header: Priority values now consistent across interfaces
add.description: Previously, three segments returned Block.priority values differing from their Segment.priority declarations. This has been fixed — all segments now use matching priority values in both places.
loadConfig() constructs a default StatusBlocksConfig in its catch branch, ensuring the return type contract is honored even when the config file is missing or malformed.

All segments use consistent early-return guards for missing data (campaign checks null status, git checks null info, usage checks null rate_limits). No dead code after returns, no redundant conditions.

resolveEffort() follows a clear source-priority cascade (transcript JSONL first, then settings.json) with explicit null fallback and caching. Each source has isolated try/catch blocks with clear intent.

add.header: Two unexplained magic constants in layout
add.description: INK_PADDING=4 is documented but the additional row1 offset of 5 is not explained. Their relationship (both compensating for Claude Code UI elements) is implicit, which confuses developers modifying layout behavior.

StatusLineData, Block, Segment, Campaign, and StatusBlocksConfig interfaces accurately model the data shapes with appropriate use of optional fields (rate_limits?, cwd?, transcript_path?) matching upstream reality.

resolveEffort() uses regex match groups and includes() checks before 'as' casts to EffortLevel, providing provably safe type narrowing. This establishes a good pattern that other modules should follow.

add.header: I/O boundary casts bypass type system
add.description: Both index.ts (stdin JSON) and config.ts (env var theme, config file JSON) use 'as' casts without runtime validation at external boundaries. The type system promises shapes that are not verified at runtime, meaning errors surface at arbitrary downstream access points rather than at the boundary.
